course_description: Covers diverse nature of organisation theory & its use in analysing & designing effective organisations. Considers many forms of organisation-level change & the many influences on it including organisational structure, technology, culture, power & organisation's external environment.

requirements.incompatible: MEBS7130 or 7501 or MGTS7080 or 7618 or 7707
requirements.assumed_background: Before attempting this course, you are advised that it is important to complete the appropriate prerequisite course(s) listed on the front of this course profile. No responsibility will be accepted by UQ Business School, the Faculty of Business, Economics and Law or The University of Queensland for poor student performance occurring in courses where the appropriate prerequisite(s) has/have not been completed, for any reason whatsoever.

timetable:
Timetables are available on the UQ Public Timetable.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The aim of this course is to provide a solid foundation in the principles and practice of organisational design and change management. Through a variety of activities, you will examine and critically evaluate a number of important elements in the design of organisations and the ways this shapes change management activities and outcomes.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify internal and external triggers for change interventions and some of the major barriers to sustainable change, considering both the individual and organisational perspective.
Explain both classic and contemporary theories and frameworks applicable to organisational design and change management.
Critically evaluate the appropriateness of different organisational designs and change interventions, with regard to the organisations internal and external context.
Formulate practical, evidence-based strategies as part of organisational (re)design or other change program, taking an ethical and nuanced approach and considering multiple stakeholders.
Work within a multi-disciplinary team as a change agent and effectively communicate recommendations to senior stakeholders.

assessment_summary.title: Change Strategies (REVISED)
assessment_summary.due_date: Article Critique:2 pm,week b4 presentation; Team Case Presentation: Wk 7,9,12; Peer Asst: 1 wk later

assessment_details.title: Change Strategies (REVISED)
assessment_details.due_date: Article Critique:2 pm,week b4 presentation; Team Case Presentation: Wk 7,9,12; Peer Asst: 1 wk later
assessment_details.task_description:
The aim of this assessment is to help you understand and apply the literature and evidence base for organisational design and change management. You will work both individually and in a team to develop your skills in developing evidence based strategies, producing outcomes collaboratively and presenting to peers and senior stakeholders.

In your tutorial in week 2 you will self-nominate into a team of 4-5 students so that there are 4 teams per tutorial group. Teams will be set up on Blackboard so that you can access the collaborative tools and for submission of your team assessment items. Teams may include members who may be remotely located. This will provide those teams with the real-world experience of working in a geographically distributed team. If there are any changes in the membership of your team, please advise your tutor immediately so we can update the teams.

Each team will be allocated 2-3 weeks of content from our Learning Activities as their focus and will support their colleagues in their learning by preparing individual article critiques of selected readings and a group case analysis and presentation relevant to these weeks. Team members will provide feedback on each other’s contribution to the overall team task through a peer assessment. The broad focus for the teams is as follows:

Team

Topic Focus

Covering

Article Critique due

Presented in tutorial:

Team 1:

Scoping & leading change

Weeks 1, 2 & 3

Wed, 2 pm, Week 6

Week 7

Team 2:

Understanding the experience of change

Weeks 4 & 5

Wed, 2 pm, Week 6

Week 7

Team 3:

Emergent Change

Weeks 6 & 7

Wed, 2 pm, Week 8

Week 9

Team 4:

Organisational Redesign

Weeks 8, 9 & 10

Wed, 2 pm, Week 11

Week 12

This assessment requires you to complete the following three (3) items.

Article Critique – 5 min Video Presentation (10%)

Individually you will prepare a 5 minute video presentation that is a critical analysis of one (1) of the required papers for your team’s assigned weeks. You can agree who covers which paper within your team but each person must cover a different paper. Your team does not need to cover all the papers if there are more than 5 papers for your allocated weeks. I suggest you chose those most relevant to your case focus. You may want to discuss your views on the papers within your team to help develop your understanding.

Students completing this aspect of the assignment remotely have the option to submit an audio file only, if the submission of a video is problematic.

Your article critique presentation should include the following aspects:

An introduction to the paper, the authors and the topic in the context of the relevant weeks material

An overview of key aspects of the article eg research questions, methodology, theories addressed and conclusions, as relevant for the type of article you chose.

Critical analysis, including comparison of key issues raised, conclusion or findings to other relevant sources such as another good quality article, reflection on relevance to your own experience, a significant case covered in the media, relevance for practitioners or your team assignment topic.

A closing summary that addresses the articles relevance to practice.

Presentation in a clear and engaging way that is well targeted to your audience. Note that the focus here is on your presentation skills and your communication of the content, not the quality of your technical production.

You may also want to speculate on relevant follow up questions, limitations of the paper or possible next steps for research to encourage those who watch your video to engage in a further conversation with you.

You will submit your video presentation is a blog post to a tutorial class Blog. This will allow your colleagues to learn from your analysis and engage with you through comments and discussions. Please also include as text in your blog post:

The full reference for your article and any other material cited, using the AMJ referencing style

A concluding paragraph summarising your assessment of the article and its usefulness for guiding evidence-based practice.

Your article critique video must be submitted through the Assessment>Change Strategies>Article Critique folder by Wednesday, 2:00 pm in the teaching week before your team presentation week. More information on how to submit your assignment is provided in this folder also. The marking team will endeavour to return feedback on your critique no later than the following Monday.

A rubric for this assessment is available on BlackBoard, but in summary the criteria for this assessment are:

Depth of knowledge and understanding of article content shown (3 marks).

Quality of critical analysis of concepts and issues (3 marks)

Professionalism of communication skills (including accurate referencing) (3 marks)

Clarity of linkage to practice. (1 mark)

You will be able to draw on a range of resources to help you, if you have not done this type of assignment before. It is very important that you practice uploading a video prior to your assignment submission. This will make life much less stressful for everyone! A session to help you with this will be presented in the lecture in Week 2. Also please refer to the additional resources provided in the Assessment>Change Strategies>Article Critique folder to cover any aspect you may be unsure of.

Team Presentation (30%) (REVISED FOR ONLINE COMPLETION)

As a team, you will prepare and present a 15 min presentation to your tutorial group on the topic allocated for your team. The aim of this part of the assessment is for you to apply the models and frameworks covered in the course and share this with your colleagues to aid their learning through an in-depth examination of a specific organisational case.

I recommend that you start working with your team on the presentation as soon as possible, as you will need to research and find a suitable case to examine. You will need to work beyond the lecture material and textbook and actively use the provided readings and your further research.

You are expected to design an interesting yet concise presentation that identifies significant insights. Remember that as a professional you will often be expected to present your experiences to senior stakeholders in a compelling way to persuade them to a course of action. This is your chance to practice these skills. You also need to demonstrate that you can draw on and apply relevant theories, frameworks and evidence from both the academic and practitioner literature. You must demonstrate your usage of at least three (3) good quality, peer reviewed, journal articles. You may, of course, use more and also relevant practitioner material.

As all tutorials will be conducted via Zoom for the rest of the semester, your presentation will also be given via Zoom. Your team should prepare your submission as a PowerPoint presentation. You can choose to present your material “live” in the Zoom tutorial or provide a video recording through the prior use of Zoom within your group or by another means.

Your submission document should include text in the “Notes” section that elaborates on the content of each slide. Overall, your notes should be a minimum of 1000 words and a maximum of 2000 words. In addition, you should also include a reference list of the sources you have drawn on in your last slide. This should be submitted as PDF document to the Assessment>Change Strategies>Case Presentation folder by 2:00 pm on the day before your presentation.

If your team has a member that has had to operate remotely from the team for the 3 weeks preceeding your presentation, please ensure that their contribution to the overall outcome is clearly indicated.

After your 15 minute presentation your tutor will facilitate a discussion to develop and consolidate the major themes in your presentation and link it to the material that we have discussed. As all Zoom tutorials will be recorded, your presentation will be available for student participating remotely to listen to and to provide input to via the tutorial discussion board.

A rubric for this assessment is available on BlackBoard, but in summary the criteria for this assessment are:

Depth of knowledge and understanding of case content shown. (8 marks)

Quality of the critical analysis of the relevant aspects of the case. (8 marks)

Integration of academic and practitioner sources. (4 marks)

Quality of the design and delivery of the presentation. (8 marks)

Professionalism of the submitted materials (2 marks).

3. Team Peer Assessment (5%)

Following your time working together, you will evaluate the contribution of each of your team members to the team project. The aim of this part of the assessment is to highlight the importance of working effectively in a team. Your individual mark will be based on the average of your team member’s assessment of your input. This assessment will be reviewed and moderated where needed to recognize those who actively and visibly supported their team members through regular and helpful interactions both in on-line forums and in the tutorial processes.

You will individually complete this assessment for your colleagues no later than 1 week after your team has presented your presentation.

The criteria for this part of the assessment are:

1: Co-ordinating: How effectively did each team member contribute to establishing the process needed to complete the assignment? Consider their contribution to setting up meeting times and work submission deadlines, aligning their work with others and keeping the team on track.

2: Communicating: How well did each team member collaborate and communicate with others in the team?

3: Co-operating: How well did each team member work within the group, responsibly completing their tasks on time, actively sharing resources and accommodating others needs when required?

4: Comforting: How well did each team member help others to maintain a positive approach to the task? Did they show empathy, provide psychological comfort and build the confidence of others?

5: Conflict resolving: Differences are inevitable in teams. How well did each team member contribute to resolving disagreements between team members? Were they able to identify sources of conflict and use effective conflict handling styles to facilitate group agreement?

You will complete your Teamwork Peer Assessment using the Teamwork Peer Assessment tool in the Assessment>Change Strategies> folder no later than one week after your presentation.

assessment_details.title: Personal learning Reflective Essay (REVISED)
assessment_details.task_description:
The aim of this assessment is to help you to integrate your learning about organisational design and change management through reflective practice. This is an essential skill for managers and organisational development professional.

INDIVIDUALLY you will prepare three (3) reflective essays addressing key aspects of your personal learning about organisational design, change management and working in teams.

In each essay, take as a focus one or two key events or interactions so far in the course, from your recent experience at work or recent observation of other organisations where you have been personally involved. Look back and briefly describe what happened and then consider your thoughts and feelings at the time and now. Step back to take into account the bigger picture and identify relevant theories, frameworks or evidence that we have discussed that help you to make sense of the situation and your response. Now write about how you can take this insight forward into new situations in the future.

You must use a minimum of one (1) good quality peer-reviewed article along with any other good quality practitioner references as needed. Please use the course reading list provided as a starting point. All sources must be cited correctly and included in a reference list according to the Academy of Management (AMJ) referencing style.

Item

Focus

Due Date

Reflection 1 (500 words, 5%)

Change at an individual level

Week 6: Mon 6th April, 2 pm

Reflection 2 (1000 words, 10%)

Emergent change

Week 9: Mon 4th May, 2 pm

Reflection 3 (1000 words, 10%)

Transformational change

Week 13: Mon 1st June, 2 pm

You will submit your reflection as a journal post within your tutorial group that will be private between yourself and the teaching team. This will allow your marker to also engage with you through comments.

A rubric for this assessment is available on BlackBoard, but in summary the criteria for this assessment are:

Content grounded in direct experience (20% of marks)

Content interpreted through relevant models (20% of marks)

Depth of self-awareness shown (20% of mark)

Focus on future action (20% of marks)

Professional writing style (20% of marks)

assessment_details.title: Change Case Analysis
assessment_details.task_description:
The aim of this report is for you to individually apply and integrate the theories, models and concepts from this course by responding to a realistic case scenario, developing recommendations and demonstrate your business writing skills by communicating your recommendation to a senior stakeholder.

In this assignment, you will take on the role of an OD consultant. You will need to analyse the current situation in the organisation and develop recommendations to take the organisation forward. Your task will be to prepare a report that would be suitable to be presented to the executive team of the case organisation.

Your submission will be structured as a consulting report but will also include evidence, models and frameworks from academic literature to explain and justify aspects of your analysis and recommendations.

The case detail will be provided at the end of Week 12, to allow discussion and clarification of the case over Week 13.

A template will be provided with guidance on the required sections. The word limit is 3000 words (including the Executive Summary but excluding references and appendices). Appendices must be referenced in-text, presented in order of that reference, and clearly necessary for you to present your recommendation.

In order to support your analysis, you must use a minimum of ten (10) good quality, peer reviewed journal articles to support your arguments. At least five (5) of these must be drawn from the MGTS3601 reading list provided. You may also draw on other good quality sources as appropriate but avoid references to textbooks. All sources must be cited correctly and included in a reference list according to the Academy of Management Journal (AMJ) referencing style.

Please note that aside from the quality of the references, what is most important is how you use the evidence from these sources to justify your analysis.

Submit your report via Turnitin in the in Assessments> Case Analysis folder by Friday, 26th June, 2:00 pm.

I highly recommend that you submit a final draft and review your TurnitIn report at least 24 hours before the assignment due date to ensure that you have not inadvertently failed to comply with the academic integrity policy. Please check your citations, quotations (these should be minimal) and references carefully.

Submission:

Via TurnitIn

learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://www.library.uq.edu.au/lr/MGTS3601 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myUQ website ( https://my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
Text Book

I have introduced a new textbook for 2020. It is a recently published book with a range of contemporary cases and practitioner insights relevant to our region. The full reference is Waddell, Creed, Cummings, & Worley. (2019). Organisational change : development and transformation (7th edition..). Melbourne, Victoria: Cengage. This book will be an important source of background information, terminology and study questions in most of our lectures and tutorials. It is available in hard copy from t he School Locker, as an e-book from the publisher, Cengage , and on-line from the library. We will not be covering every topic in the book, and the material we discuss will be supplemented by the additional material from the recommended readings list.

Recommended Readings List

I do not expect you to read every article on this list in detail...though I would be delighted! Please see these papers as a resource for use to extend your understanding, draw on for your assignments and to explore the aspects that you are particularly interested in. They are a mix of classic and contemporary scholarly and practitioner sources and I have chosen each one carefully to illustrate key aspects of organisational design and change management. I hope at least some of them will inspire your interest as well.

Practitioner Resources

The field of organisational design and organisational change is extremely dynamic and most books date quickly. It is therefore important to read, think, and explore further than the prescribed readings. Students are expected to be current on how the issues in this course are are seen in actual organisations. Therefore, I encourage you to subscribe to some key practitioner sites for current news, case studies and ideas. These might include:

The Australian Human Resource Institute: www.ahri.com.au

McKinsey & Company https://www.mckinsey.com/business-functions/organization/our-insights

Deloitte Human Capital Practice https://www2.deloitte.com/au/en/pages/human-capital/topics/human-capital

Harvard Business Review on change management and other human capital issues https://hbr.org/topic/change-management

Learn (Blackboard)

All courses are supported by the UQ Learning Management System, Blackboard Learn, which provides an interactive environment for the students and teachers. This is accessible via PC or Mac or Mobile (Apps) environment.

To log on to Blackboard, go to https://learn.uq.edu.au.

As Blackboard is a web‐based application, you will need a recently‐released browser for your computer. For the latest Release Notes for Bb Student, see http://en-gb.help.blackboard.com/Bb_Student/z_Release_Notes_Bb_Student .

For help with Learn.UQ (Blackboard), see https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it/learnuq-blackboard-help/learnuq-blackboard-contacts or contact AskUs https://web.library.uq.edu.au/contact-us or telephone 334 64312.

learning_activities.topic: Wed 14:00 Week 1: Course overview & preparations : Topics: Course aims, learning approach, assessment, defining key terms in for studying organisations and change, hard truths and myths about change. Learning Objectives: 2 Readings/Ref: Waddell (Ch 1); Nadler (Ch 2); By 2005; Hughes

Wed 14:00 Week 2: Types of Organisational Change : Planned organisational change vs emergent change. Strategic change vs operational change. Personal change vs organisational change. Issues raised by triggers of change, scope, scale and timeframe of change. Preparing and submitting a video assignment.
Wed - Thu Experiences of Change : Introductions, assignment team formation, working with the literature Learning Objectives: 5

Wed 14:00 Week 3: Change Leadership and Change Agents : Understanding the role of leaders, managers, consultants and the HR team in change. Key competencies, values and ethical perspectives. Learning Objectives: 3, 5 Readings/Ref: Waddell (Ch 3); Baran; Burnes; Di Stefano; Oreg 2019
Wed - Thu Change Agent Skills : Change agents skills and competencies, learning by reflection. Learning Objectives: 5 Readings/Ref: Di Stefano
Mon - Sun TEACHING PAUSE

Wed 14:00 Week 4: The Human Side of Change : Understanding culture, power, resistance to and embrace of change. Emotions, secrecy, silence and the challenges of sustaining change. Learning Objectives: 1, 2 Readings/Ref: Waddell (Ch 4); Clausen; Georgalis; Oreg 2016; Prochaska; Rafferty
Wed - Thu The Human Side of Change : Understanding stakeholders Learning Objectives: 1, 4

Wed 14:00 Week 5: Understanding Organisations in Context : Tools and approaches for diagnosing organisational issues, readiness for change, understanding stakeholders, communication and designing interventions. Learning Objectives: 3, 4 Readings/Ref: Waddell (Ch 5); Endrejat; Judge; McFillen; Nielsen; Raza
Wed - Thu Diagnosing Organisations : Change readiness assessment Learning Objectives: 4

Wed 14:00 Week 6: Emergent Change: Learning & Innovation : Defining learning organisations. Understanding process consultation as an intervention model. Working with teams for innovation and performance. Learning Objectives: 2, 3, 4 Readings/Ref: Waddell (Ch 6, Ch 7); Aranda; Argyris; Jemine
Wed - Thu Tools for change interventions : Communication strategies Learning Objectives: 4
Mon - Sun MID SEMESTER BREAK
Mon - Sun MID SEMESTER BREAK

Wed 14:00 Week 7: Emergent Change: Strategy and Structure : Understanding the environment. Responding to external change, working across organisations, operational level restructuring, re-engineering and work redesign. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3 Readings/Ref: Waddell (Ch 8); Klehe; Seppälä; Wee

Wed - Thu CASE TEAM 2 Presentation : The impact of change on employees. Team presentation and facilitated discussion. Learning Objectives: 3, 4, 5 Wed - Thu CASE TEAM 1 Presentation : The scope and scale of change, role of change leaders and change agents. Team presentation and facilitated discussion Learning Objectives: 3, 4, 5

Wed 14:00 Week 8: Organisational Transformation : Strategic change and organisational design. Learning Objectives: 1, 2 Readings/Ref: Waddell (Ch 9); Alfes; Nadler (Ch 2); Bartunek; Cohee; Dalpiaz; Jordan; Raynard
Wed - Thu Tools for change interventions : Change impact assessment, intervention design Learning Objectives: 4

Wed 14:00 Week 9: Understanding Organisational Design : Types of organisational design, structural levers, organisational lifecycles, culture and systems. Learning Objectives: 2, 3 Readings/Ref: Nadler (Ch 5); Bernstein (See Wk 9 folder); Burton; Dunbar
Wed - Thu CASE TEAM 3 Presentation : Innovation and change at the team level. Team presentation and facilitated discussion. Learning Objectives: 3, 4, 5

Wed 14:00 Week 10: Redesigning Organisations for Growth : The organisational redesign process, understanding strategic, structural and informal linking. Learning Objectives: 2, 3 Readings/Ref: Nadler (Ch 9); Raisch; Schaubroeck
Wed - Thu Redesigning for growth : Case Simulation (Round 1) Learning Objectives: 3, 4 Readings/Ref: Bernstein

Wed 14:00 Week 11: Implementing Organisational Redesigns : Organisational design meets organisational change. Interventions for people, process and technology. Learning Objectives: 2, 3, 4 Readings/Ref: Nadler (Ch 10); Luo
Wed - Thu Redesigning for growth : Case simulation (Round 2) Debrief and reflection. Learning Objectives: 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Bernstein
Wed 14:00 Week 12: Change in Transnational Contexts : Multinational organisations, cross-cultural competence Learning Objectives: 1, 3, 5 Readings/Ref: Waddell (Ch 10); Balogun; Hernes
Wed - Thu CASE TEAM 4 presentation : Organisational restructuring. Team presentation and facilitated debriefing Learning Objectives: 3, 4, 5
Wed 14:00 Week 13: Integrative review : Review, case analysis and exam preparation Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4 Readings/Ref: Di Stefano
Wed - Thu Group Case Analysis : Case analysis and learning reflection Learning Objectives: 4, 5

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Tutorial - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Tutorial (AIT) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. All students are strongly encouraged to complete the module. Some course coordinators may require you to complete the module as part of your assessment.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

School of Business
Assessment Guidelines

Student Notices are posted progressively on the UQ Business School website http://www.business.uq.edu.au/student-notices

Reviewing Assessment

The Assessment Viewing Register procedures are:

Go to Current Student – Student Notices – Exam Viewing – online form here then log in.

Students should log in using their normal UQ name (e.g. s9990000) and password.

Policy on Consultation for Progressive Assessment

Whilst staff members are genuinely sympathetic to assessment grievances, individual reviews of progressive assessment are not feasible on a large scale. Accordingly, following the release of marks, consultation will be strictly limited as follows:

Students will be seen only in designated consultation hours, or by appointment.

No consultation will occur on the day of the release of marks.

The consultation period will be limited.

Priority at all times will be given to those students who did not achieve the pass mark for that piece of assessment.

Perusal of Final Examination Papers

Students are permitted to peruse their final examination papers but should note that this is not an opportunity to seek remarking of papers.

The sessions are scheduled during weeks 1 and 2 of the teaching week in both semester one and two; and in the case of summer examinations during weeks 1 and 2 in the following semester one.

The only opportunity for students to view their final exam paper is during the above sessions. Exam papers cannot be viewed by approaching individual staff outside of these times, except for prospective graduands.

Students completing their final courses and graduating may wish to see their final exam before graduation.

Such cases are handled on a case-by-case basis with the individual staff member.

The student will be given their exam paper and the suggested answers (or the marking guide) which they may view on site.

No exam papers may be taken out of the viewing room.

No bags may be taken into the viewing room.

At the time of viewing the exam papers, the Course Coordinator or their authorised delegate must be available in the viewing room.

Appeal Against Results

Should a student feel that the mark allocated does not reflect the standard of her/his work, then she/he is encouraged to discuss the matter with the Course Coordinator. In the event of course-based disputes not being resolved with the Course Coordinator, the student may appeal to the relevant Head of School and then the Associate Dean Academic of the BEL Faculty.

Re-marks

A student who believes the judgement of their work and the resulting mark or grade they were awarded for a piece of assessment does not reflect his/her performance as measured against the published assessment criteria, can apply for a re-mark.

The result of re-marking should not unfairly disadvantage other students.

A re-mark will be conducted once only on the piece of assessment.

In all cases, a re-mark will replace the original mark for the piece of assessment in the calculation of the final grade which could result in a higher or lower grade.

Grounds for consideration

An application for a re-mark is considered only when the student:

has sought and received feedback about their performance on the assessment from the course coordinator or lecturer concerned (or has viewed the piece of assessment and, where available, model answers/written comments or other feedback/etc); and

after receiving feedback, provides a sound written academic case to demonstrate how the mark or grade awarded for an entire task, or a discrete component of a multi-component task, does not reflect their performance against the advertised criteria and standards for that work. Students should reference the published assessment criteria for the piece of assessment and clearly show where they believe there are grounds for reconsideration of the assessment judgement.

The desire for extra marks to achieve a higher final course grade is not sufficient grounds for a re-mark.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Other
School of Business
Guidelines

Computing Facilities

Courses offered by UQ Business School utilise the internet and other computer facilities. Students should be familiar with these resources prior to undertaking a course. AskUs, available through the University of Queensland Library, offers computing help and training free to UQ students. Contact the AskUs Desk, Level 2 Duhig Bldg at St Lucia.

Professional Code of Conduct

The University of Queensland has strict guidelines around academic integrity, plagiarism, and assessment which should be carefully read in the policy sections of this ECP. In addition, UQ Business School expects that as future business leaders and entrepreneurs, our students will adhere to the highest standards of ethical behaviour in all of their coursework.

Attendance at lectures, tutorials, seminars and workshops is expected and students are reminded that lecture recordings are not designed to replace in-class interactions and learning experiences.

It is a common courtesy to your fellow students and teaching staff to ensure you are on time for all class sessions, that you come prepared and are ready and willing to actively participate in class activities. Leaving class early is also disruptive to your fellow students and lecturers/tutors.

Please use professional etiquette and courtesy in all of your written and oral communication with teaching staff and fellow students. Please also ensure that all written correspondence includes your name and student ID.

Another matter of etiquette is the use of phones, laptops and other electronic devices in class. Some in-class activities may require the use of devices but otherwise we expect that these will be switched off during class time.

Students are not to make their own recordings of lectures or tutorials as this contravenes the UQ policy on Student Integrity and Misconduct. This includes taking photos of lecture or tutorial slides and/or other materials. Teaching staff will distribute all required materials to you via Blackboard.

Course Coordinators and lecturers may set additional requirements and expectations for their respective courses.

Other Course Guidelines

AN IMPORTANT MESSAGE ABOUT ATTENDANCE

Any absences from the lectures and tutorials in this course will be to your educational disadvantage as well as jeopardising your groups tutorial participation assessment. I know you have heard this message before. But in this course it is true because the CONTENT OF LECTURES IS EXAMINABLE AND THIS CONTENT IS NOT DUPLICATED OUTSIDE THE LECTURE. There is another reason to have you along each week. The program moves quickly and absences expose you to the risk of falling behind. For some students this could be fatal, for others it will disrupt their developing comprehension.

Attend all lectures

Attend all tutorials

Particpate in the individual and group presentations in your tutorial at your allocated time.

If you do not complete your individual presentation or absent yourself from your group presentation that will make you ineligible to pass the course as you have not met all the assessment requirements.

University of Queensland's values and guiding principles

The University of Queensland Business School is committed to establishing and sustaining a community that supports independent scholarly learning, critical judgment, academic integrity and ethical standards in all students.

As a member of the University community, we also expect students to accept shared responsibility, conducting themselves in a manner consistent with the University’s values and guiding principles.

In accordance with the Student Charter and Student Integrity & Misconduct policy , students must ensure they:

(a) are familiar and compliant with the expectations and responsibilities prescribed under the student charter ;

(b) submit only work that is their own or that properly acknowledges the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of others;

(c) avoid lending original work to others for any reason;

(d) comply with assessment conditions for policies and procedures, rules and regulations;

(e) be clear about what is appropriate referencing and the consequences of inappropriate referencing in their discipline;

(f) do not collude with people, including fellow students and external third-party services, when completing assessment work;

(g) complete the academic integrity online tutorial within the first semester of study at the University prior to the census date.

If you do require support with your studies, please contact your Course Coordinator, tutor or UQ Student Services .

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
Globalization, technology change, economic uncertainty and changing expectations of employers, employees and consumers have placed enormous pressures on organizations. Organizations have responded to these pressures in a number of ways including changing their business models, disrupting other’s business models, outsourcing, introducing automation, democratising decision-making and encouraging greater collaboration and knowledge sharing.

Some say that 70% of all change initiatives fail. While that figure is debatable, implementing sustainable change in an increasingly volatile and complex business environment is not easy. Our job this semester is to understand some of the fundamental theories and frameworks covering organisational design and organisational change and how these can be applied in the real world to shape better outcomes. More effective organisations and more sustainable change leads to healthier, more productive workplaces. This is a benefit to all stakeholders: employees, shareholders, customers and society as a whole.

Why is this important for you? Because the work environment you will enter after graduation is now heavily influenced by change, if you are not experiencing this already. Building your understanding of the many factors that influence and shape change efforts and organisational effectiveness within the workplace will benefit you, firstly as an employee. As an HR professional, a manager or an entrepreneur, you will need to apply the tools that we will learn to optimise organisational designs and manage change in your own organisations.

The ability to critically evaluate material presented to you in group settings, both as reports and presentations, such as may occur at management meetings, is a key skill required of managers in today's business environment. These are skills which this course will help you to acquire.

Organisations today expect graduates to be able to translate the theories learned at university into meaningful every day practice. This course has been designed to give you both experience in presenting your own research findings as well as the opportunity to reflect upon and respond to applications of change and organisational design tools and the presentations of your peers in tutorials.

The potential impact of change on people and economies is significant. On the plus side, change can produce enormous opportunities for growth and development. On the negative side, ineffective change processes can derail organisations or teams, produce survivor syndrome, decreased organisational commitment, job disengagement and reduced staff creativity. Poorly conceived or led change can also have disastrous economic effects for the organisation. This course will help you to understand how to best navigate the upside and downside of change and change initiatives.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

There have been some changes to the material to be presented in this course to increase the coverage and practical application of organisational design practices. A new text book and updated reading list has also been provided. The presentation of article critiques has been changed to be a brief video, instead of a presentation in tutorial time. This will free up tutorial time for active application of the material learned and more in-depth discussion in small groups. Students will still have access to their colleagues critiques and be able to interact and discuss them via a tutorial group blog. The mid-semester quiz has been replaced by three short reflective essays to encourage integration and application of the material covered and to develop reflective practice skills. Detailed rubrics for each assignment will be provided to help students have a better understanding of the requirements of each assessment.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
Learning is a dynamic process, and development of evidence-based expertise requires adapting the content and timing of learning activities to learner characteristics. Thus, you should note that the lecture and tutorial program outlined is a guide to the material to be covered in the course and not a rigid schedule for when and what material will be covered. Any changes to the timing and focus on specific material will be mutually decided in class based on student learning needs.

Lecture + Tutorial Approach [REVISED]

Lecture content will be made available as a series of recorded videos for each week of content. These will be available by 6 pm Tuesday in each week. I would expect you to take the opportunity to view them prior to your tutorial, as a minimum, along with the required reading. You will get much more value from the material if you have read the textbook chapter and, in particular, noted any key terms. You will gain even more if you have read the abstracts for the recommended readings. I will offer a weekly interactive session, covering applications of the key points from this week, as long as there is demand from you for this type of support. I encourage you to use the Blackboard discussion forum tool between classes for questions and to further develop your thinking. I will encourage you to reflect on your learning regularly and to relate new learning to your prior experience on a week by week basis. I will also be regularly asking you to give me feedback on how the learning process is working for you.

Tutorials will be offered on-line in an interactive format. They are designed to be help you apply the material covered in the lectures, with a particular focus on the practical change management and organisational design tools and methods. The other part of the tutorial process is the opportunity to both present to and learn from your peers in analysing cases based on real organisations. This will continue in this new format.

With the travel restrictions as a result of COVID-19 we will all be working remotely and in some cases have colleagues joining in from different timezones. This is a normal part of working in contemporary organisations. I encourage everyone to generously embrace this learning opportunity and to support their team members and colleagues - whether located locally or remotely.

Student Responsibility

In accord with student learning as a joint responsibility of student and instructor along with the Professional Code of Conduct and Professional Communication Standards (see below), please let me know immediately if you have any major course-related concerns so that I can make any necessary adjustments to ensure that learning outcomes are being achieved throughout the semester.

It is also your responsibility to attend classes and consult designated notice boards and websites for correct and up-to-date information regarding the course. Relying on fellow students to pass on information may result in incorrect information for which the University will not be held responsible.

Professional Code of Conduct

Your success in this course derives from the same kinds of behaviours and attitudes that will make you successful in your careers. As business professionals, leaders and entrepreneurs, you will be expected to adhere to high standards of ethical behaviour and hard work. The work habits you develop and demonstrate in this course will make an important contribution to your future success. What constitutes a proper code of conduct includes:

Please respect all students’ and lecturers’ time by doing your best at all times. This means, for instance, coming on time and prepared for class. The focus of the course will not be on finding the “right answer” to a given problem, but rather on a discussion and an “it depends” attitude. This demands that you contribute actively, assume responsibility for your actions, meet deadlines, graciously accept written and oral feedback, and demonstrate respect of self and others.

If the problem of free-riders occurs in your assignment team, please deal with this issue immediately with your group member. To excel as professionals, business leaders and entrepreneurs, you will need to deal openly, constructively, and in a timely manner with any issues in your team. Therefore, excuses made afterwards will not be accepted.

​Cheating, plagiarism or re-use of other people's material will not be tolerated under any circumstances. In order to eliminate cheating at UQ we adopt the Turnitin system on Blackboard, which cross-checks your submissions with literature and other texts. Any assignments where indicates of cheating or plagiarism are found are reported to the course examiner for further examination.

Please discuss any comments on the course as soon as possible with the course coordinator so that we may be able to take them into account as soon as possible. The responsibility for a good course is a mutual commitment and therefore also lies in your hands.

Professional Communication Standards

Professionalism requires professional communication skills and professional dress. All assignments and class discussions must be presented in a professional manner. Attention to using non-sexist and non-stigmatising language is essential. Intercultural communication sensitivity is expected at all times. All assignments must be carefully copy-edited prior to submission.

Administration

All content related questions and specific questions regarding the administration of this course should be directed to me as the course coordinator. Please include the course code MGTS3601 and a brief statement of the topic in your e-mail subject. Please note that the turnaround time for e-mail may be up to 48 hours as I may be working on other matters.

General administrative questions (for example centrally timetabled exams, room venues, etc) may be addressed to BEL Student Centre: Level 2, Colin Clark Building #39, St Lucia Campus.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment

late_submission:
Students must fill in an Application for Extension of Assessment Due Date and provide supporting documentation to Assessment, Examinations & Misconducts Coordinator https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/applying-extension at least 24 hours prior to the submission date.

Your application must be submitted at the location and by the due date specified in Section 5.3 of the relevant Electronic Course Profile (ECP).

Supporting documentation such as a medical certificate, funeral notice etc. must be provided. Scanned or photographed copies should be attached to your email.

For an application on medical grounds, the medical practitioner must not be a near relative or close associate. Examples of near relatives are partner, child, brother, sister, parent. Examples of close associates are close friends, neighbours and partners or children of colleagues.

Extension criteria are applied consistently for equity reasons.

You may discuss your situation with your course coordinator, but you still need to make a formal extension request using this form.

Applications on medical grounds will be approved for the number of calendar days the medical certificate indicates you were unfit for study. You are expected to act in a timely manner and must make an appointment as soon as your condition impacts on your ability to study.

If you have a continuing condition you should contact Student Services to arrange a Student Access Plan(Disability) [SAPD]. You must still submit the application form

Non-permissible circumstances

Extensions will not be granted where the School is not satisfied you took reasonable measures to avoid the circumstances that contributed to you not submitting by the due date. The following are not grounds for an extension:

holiday arrangements (including overseas travel);

misreading a due date;

social and leisure events;

moving house;

pressure of work/competing deadlines;

computer issues.

The due dates for assignments must be adhered to and you are reminded to keep a copy of your work, in case of lost assignments or disputes over grading.

You will incur penalties if your work is submitted late (i.e. after the due date and without an approved extension).

Items (for which no extension has been granted) submitted after the due date and time, incur a late submission penalty. The penalty is at the rate of 5% of the total available marks for that particular piece of assessment, for each calendar day or part thereof that the item is overdue. The penalty once calculated is deducted from the marks awarded for the assessment. Assessment submitted more than 10 days after the due date will receive zero marks.
other_assessment_information:
Supplementary Assessment

Should you fail a course with a grade of 3, or a non-graded ‘N’, you may be eligible for supplementary assessment. Refer to my.UQ for information on supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment may not be available for all courses, or for some of the assessment items for a course. The highest grade you can receive following supplementary assessment is a 4 or P. Details of availability of supplementary assessment for this course are set out below.

Supplementary Assessment is available for this Course .

Refer to my.UQ for how to apply for supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment can take any form, for example, an oral or a written exam. Students who are eligible and approved for a supplementary assessment and the form of assessment is an examination, are expected to be available to sit the supplementary exam during the University’s Deferred and Supplementary examination period. Once approved, supplementary assessment cannot be rescinded by the student.

Other Supplementary Assessment Information

For all UQ Business School courses supplementary assessment is weighted at 100% and covers the learning outcomes for the course. This means that the marks obtained for any progressive assessment throughout the semester are disregarded and the final result for the course is based entirely on the performance in the supplementary assessment.

Other

ASSIGNMENT SUBMISSION

Unless otherwise advised by your course coordinator, all written assignments are to be electronically submitted using Blackboard (Learn) https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . The instructions for submission below can also be found on the "Assessment" link in Blackboard. The online submission is in addition to any other submission requirements that appear in this eCP.

IMPORTANT: Online Submission

Ensure you receive a Turnitin submission ID or Learn.UQ confirmation number, and retain this number as proof of submission. If you do not receive proof of submission, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful and take steps to address the issue. It is your responsibility to ensure the assignment has been successfully submitted. Any requests to waive the late submission penalty without proof of submission will not be considered under any circumstances.

The method of submission will be decided by your Course Coordinator, either Turnitin or Blackboard. Upload your assignment using the relevant link created for your use .

INSTRUCTIONS FOR SUBMITTING YOUR ASSIGNMENT & CONFIRMING YOUR SUBMISSION

TURNITIN

Submit your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1921/0#0

Upload your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1921/1#1

Review and submit your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1921/2#2

Confirm your submission - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1921/3#3

BLACKBOARD

Access your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it/learnuq-blackboard-help/learnuq-assessment/blackboard-assignments/assignment-submission-instructions

Submit your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1912/1#1

Add comments - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1912/2#2

Submit - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1912/3#3

Review/resubmit - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1912/4#4

For ALL, individual and Team assessment items:

The assignment must be submitted electronically using the online submission link located in the Blackboard Assessment menu https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . Hardcopy submissions are not required except for items that are too difficult to upload through the online submission links eg posters and multi-modal assessment tasks.

FOR TEAM ASSIGNMENTS

All team assignments must have ONE nominated representative's details at the top of the coversheet, with details of other team members listed on the lower section.

Students should keep a list of their team members' full names and contact details.

Please check with your course coordinator if online submission is required for a team assignment.

REFERENCING AND CITING

Assignments must be substantially your own work. If you wish to report another author's point of view you should do so in your own words, and properly footnote the reference in accordance with the school style. Direct quotations should be used sparingly, form a small part of your work, and must be placed in quotation marks and referenced.

Any material taken from texts and other references, including electronic resources, CD-ROMS, and the Internet, must be acknowledged using the accepted School style.

Students are encouraged to discuss issues that arise in this course together, however, the written work you submit must be entirely your own . Similarly, you must not help another student to cheat by lending assignments (present or past).

For more information on referencing styles, visit the library or see: https://web.library.uq.edu.au/research-tools-techniques/referencing/referencing-style-guides .

If you do not reference correctly, you could be found guilty of academic misconduct. Please see this link for more information: https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/student-integrity-and-conduct/academic-integrity-and-student-conduct.

For more information on assessment, please review the UQ Assessment Policy at https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment

DEFERRED EXAMS

Midsemester Examinations

A student who fails to sit the midsemester examination and/or the quizzes at the scheduled times will be awarded a mark of zero, unless the School receives a deferred request on Si-net with appropriate documentation satisfactorily explaining the student's absence. The application must be submitted either prior to the scheduled date of the midsemester exam, or no later than five days after the midsemester examination. All deferred midsemester examinations within UQ Business School will take place on Saturday 2 May 2020 . A timetable for the deferred midsemester examinations will be available on 24 April 2020 .

This will be the only opportunity to take the deferred midsemester examination. Any student who is granted a deferred midsemester examination and who fails to attend at the appointed time and place will be awarded a mark of zero as per the PPL rules. There is no opportunity to defer a deferred examination under any circumstances.

The process for applying for deferred midsemester exams is as follows:

Applications for deferred midsemester examinations must be made online through Si-Net. Information about the process of applying and the online form can be found via mySi-net > myRequests > Deferred Examination Requests .

You will receive an email from Si-net to your student email account indicating whether or not your application has been approved.

Be aware of Discretionary exam request – if you use the discretionary deferred option, it will NOT be available to you again.

If you are applying on medical grounds or other eligible circumstances DO NOT use the Discretionary option.

The timetable will be found on the UQ Business School webpage > Student > Student Notices > under Deferred Midsemester Examination (see http://www.business.uq.edu.au/student-notices )

Students who have three or more deferred exams will be notified by the Examinations Section to put in an Alternative Arrangements form.

Students who have two deferred exams will have a choice of when to sit both exams.

Any enquiries regarding midsemester deferred examinations should be directed to the Assessment, Examinations and Misconducts Coordinator, exams.officer@business.uq.edu.au .

Midsemester examination dates:

Friday 27 March 2020

Monday 6 April 2020

Final Examinations

A student who is unable to sit a scheduled final examination must apply for a deferred examination no later than 5 days after the date the original examination was held.

The process for applying for deferred final exams is as follows:

Applications for deferred final examinations must be made online through Si-Net. Information about the process of applying and the online form can be found via mySi-net > myRequests > Deferred Examination Requests .

You will receive an email from Si-net to your student email account indicating whether or not your application has been approved.

The Examination Section and Faculties may approve applications for deferred examinations under the broad headings medical, compassionate or exceptional circumstances. Deferred examinations are not automatically granted. For further information (such as evidence required and time limits) please see My.UQ https://my.uq.edu.au/ .
